title: Sourdough discard crumpets
subtitle: Crisp-edged crumpets made from unfed sourdough discard
excerpt: Thick, spongy crumpets cooked on a griddle, made from sourdough discard thinned with milk and lifted with a little bicarbonate of soda. Riddled with holes, crisp around the edge, and ready for butter.
sourceNotes: Crumpets are documented in English culinary literature from the seventeenth century as a griddle-baked batter bread. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg, records a yeast-raised batter bread from which modern crumpets descend. The sourdough discard variant follows the same principle: a fermented batter, aerated with bicarbonate of soda just before cooking.

content.text: Crumpets depend on a loose, fermented batter that forms bubbles when it hits a hot surface, then sets around them to leave the characteristic holes. Sourdough

is a natural fit: the unfed starter provides fermented flavour and a small amount of leavening, supplemented here by a pinch of bicarbonate of soda added just before cooking. The result has a better flavour than crumpets made with commercial yeast alone.

You do not need rings to make crumpets at home, though they give a neater result. Without rings, the batter spreads a little more; the crumpets are shallower and more irregular, but just as good. Cook them on a dry, non-stick pan or well-seasoned griddle over a medium-low heat. Patience with the heat is the skill: too high and the bases burn before the tops set.

attrs.prepNote: about half a teaspoon; add just before cooking

content.text: Whisk {{sourdough-starter}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} together until smooth. The batter should be thick and pourable, similar to double cream. Cover and leave to rest at room temperature for 30 minutes. This short rest allows the flour to hydrate fully and lets the batter settle.

Heat a heavy-based non-stick frying pan or griddle over medium-low heat for two minutes. Stir {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} into the batter just before you start cooking; the batter will bubble slightly. Lightly grease the pan with a small piece of kitchen paper dipped in vegetable oil. Spoon about 60 ml of batter per crumpet into the pan, spacing them well apart. Cook without moving for 7 to 9 minutes, until the surface is covered with holes and the top has set and lost its wet look. Flip and cook for a further 2 minutes to colour the top lightly. The crumpet should be springy in the centre and a deep golden-brown on the first side.
Serve immediately, or toast briefly in a toaster before serving. A generous amount of unsalted butter is traditional; it pools in the holes as it melts.
The bicarbonate of soda must be added immediately before cooking, not mixed in ahead. It begins reacting on contact with the acidic starter and the leavening dissipates quickly.
If the batter is too thick the holes won't form properly. Thin with a splash more milk, a tablespoon at a time, until the batter pours like heavy cream.
Crumpet rings (7 cm pastry rings or English muffin rings) placed in the pan give a uniform shape. Grease them lightly before each crumpet.
